start_door.name: door
start_door.description: A heavy door with no handle or lock. There is an engraving above it.

specialDesc.text: The heavy door to the south stands open.

description.text: I wouldn't be able to see the keyboard, even if it wasn't dark - my head is rigidly pointing straight ahead.
I can't see much of the keyboard through the rotating mirror.
A keyboard attached to the side of the wheel. It seems I'm meant to type on it.

typeResponses.text: There is another noise like a breaker being switched, and blinding light floods the room.[PAUSE]
Nothing happens. Perhaps I need to use a command word first?

At first I think the solution wasn't right, but then the wheel sets me to a more sensible orientation towards the ground, and the metal fittings snap free. I slump to the floor.[PAUSE]Now that I'm no longer spinning, I can see the room more clearly. It's rectangular in shape, with one long wall being taken up with the mirror, and the other containing twelve boxes. The wheel stands in the centre. There is a door on one of the short sides of the room.
I hear the sound of a latch releasing. The door opens.

description.text: It's hard to see much of the wheel from here, but through the mirror I can see it has five spokes, each spitting fire, and a terrified individual pinned to the centre.
The wheel that I was previously bound to. It has five spokes, slightly singed at the ends, and a platform for someone to stand. A keyboard is attached to the side.
A mirror covering the entire wall opposite you. In it you can see yourself, lashed to the wheel. There is writing scrawled across it.
A mirror covering the entire wall. There is writing scrawled across it.

mirror_writing.name: mirror writing
mirror_writing.description: This sentence is scrawled across the mirror: [I]You're bound to a wheel! I mark the spot with charge.[/I]
mirror_writing.readDesc: [I]You're bound to a wheel! I mark the spot with charge.[/I]

lockbox_grid.name: boxes
lockbox_grid.description: A 4x4 grid of lock boxes set into the wall behind the wheel. Each has a small dial with letters on it. The upper three rows are out of reach from here.

door_writing.name: door engraving
door_writing.description: Writing engraved into the wall above the door:[I]Your prison: two rats, being backward, are lost with us.

description.text: Two shafts of light illuminate two lecterns. I can't see where the light comes from, the source is far too bright.
Now that the main lights are on, I can't see the spotlights.

lectern_a.name: left lectern
lectern_a.description: A wooden lectern, lit from above by a spotlight. It has a newspaper article attached to it, which I can just make out from here.

lectern_b.name: right lectern
lectern_b.description: A wooden lectern, lit from above by a spotlight. It has a a block of text written out by what looks like a typewriter. It is titled "Cross Words".

lectern_a_writing.name: article
lectern_a_writing.description: The newspaper article, somewhat yellowed with age, is pasted down onto the left lectern. I can just make out the text.
lectern_a_writing.readDesc:
The newspaper article is titled "No Cross Words for New Holder" and is dated to 1997.

[I]This month the prestigious periodical "Puzzle Quarterly" will see its editorship change hands. Under the creative control of "Parrot" over the last twenty years, this initially niche magazine has seen its readership blossom, and now regularly reaches 80,000 eager readers. "Holder" certainly has big shoes to fill. Profoundly private, we caught up with the new editor not over a coffee, but by "e-mail".

We asked Holder how he first got into puzzles.

"When I was young, I was mostly entertained via correspondence chess and correspondence puzzles. As a home-schooled child, I had many friends with whom to correspond - other home-schooled children. We would set each other elaborate puzzles where everything in the envelope played a part, even how the address was written on the front."

From these humble yet strange origins, Holder has grown to become the most in-demand setter in the puzzle world. Known for his dense, linguistically-focussed puzzles referencing literature and mythology from across the ages, he has been known to flumox many a puzzle completer. We leave the last word to Holder themselves:

"It is a joy to be able to do this for my living. It is a meagre living, but worthwhile."[/I]

lectern_b_writing.name: document
lectern_b_writing.description: A single piece of paper with typewritten text sits on the lectern, titled "Cross Words". I can just about read it from here.
lectern_b_writing.readDesc:
[FIRST]The piece of paper has a short message, which reads as follows:

[FIRST END][I]Hello my dear boy. You may be wondering where you are. You may be wondering what is happening to you. You don't know me. Well, you know "Holder", but you don't know me. But I do know you. I wonder if in all your setting you've had to solve a puzzle like this? Either way, I shouldn't say too much, as there is much to come. And I'd rather leave you in the dark - ha ha.

Why don't you lat in the light? God said it in the beginning, on the first day. Flat, you take 9 and mess it up.[/I][FIRST]

Holder? I remember them as a setter when I was just getting started. Have I been kidnapped by a crossword setter?[FIRST END]
